statuses.labeling: current official local resources are not sufficient to confirm one comprehensive nationwide deepfake-labeling mandate. Labeling claims remain context-level rather than article-level in this corpus, so services should run internal labeling standards as provisional controls and defer hard-law certainty claims until expanded official sources are available. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.election: explicit evidence of a stand-alone election-deepfake statute remains limited in the current corpus. Election-risk governance should be handled conservatively through broader criminal/misinformation/public-order control channels until dedicated official election-law sources are added. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.non_consensual: this is the strongest identifiable codification track in the current German set. Criminal-law treatment of non-consensual deepfake/personality-rights harm is materially visible, supporting stronger statements in this dimension compared with others. (Ref2)
statuses.provider_liability: article-level platform-duty codification cannot be finalized from current corpus alone. Platform controls should therefore emphasize robust internal governance and auditable response logic while official legal-source coverage is expanded. (Ref1)
statuses.penalty: a criminal sanctions track is identifiable, but final article wording, commencement timing, and enacted-text certainty still require latest federal publication cross-checks. Best current framing is: codification direction is clear; final enacted precision is pending confirmation. (Ref2, Ref1)

statuses.labeling: no law in the current corpus: no official source has been added confirming a general deepfake-labeling or watermarking mandate.
statuses.election: no law in the current corpus: no official election deepfake source has been added.
statuses.non_consensual: no law in the current corpus: no confirmed official NCII deepfake source has been added.
statuses.provider_liability: no law in the current corpus: no confirmed platform-duty source has been added for deepfake services.
statuses.penalty: no law in the current corpus: no confirmed penalty source has been added.

statuses.labeling: no dedicated law establishes a general deepfake-labeling mandate in the current Mexico corpus. The performer-rights reform is more about contractual consent/conditions/remuneration than public labeling of generated media. (Ref2, Ref3)
statuses.election: no dedicated law for election deepfakes is identified in the current Mexico files. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3)
statuses.non_consensual: the criminal-code bill directly targets AI-created intimate content, but it remains proposal-stage in this corpus. The enacted labor/copyright reform strengthens consent-based control over image/voice use for performers. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3)
statuses.provider_liability: no dedicated law establishes a platform-duty regime for deepfake services in the current files. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3)
statuses.penalty: draft/proposal-stage and weak: criminal sanctions for AI intimate content are proposed in the Federal Criminal Code amendment and should remain weak/proposed until official enactment evidence is added. (Ref1)

statuses.labeling: with current official corpus scope, it is difficult to conclude that a single nationwide comprehensive deepfake-labeling mandate is fully codified. Labeling controls likely operate through mixed channels (federal baseline + state rules + platform policy), so implementation should be designed conservatively and be update-ready by jurisdiction. (Ref1)
statuses.election: a standalone federal election-deepfake statute cannot be confidently established from current corpus alone. Election governance should therefore be framed as distributed control across federal baseline duties and state-level election/misinformation enforcement pathways. (Ref1)
statuses.non_consensual: this is the strongest codified area in the current US set. The TAKE IT DOWN Act functions as a federal reference point for non-consensual intimate synthetic-harm response, while practical enforcement intensity can vary by state framework and platform execution quality. (Ref1)
statuses.provider_liability: provider/platform liability is not a single-code federal model in this corpus; it is a layered responsibility model across legal and policy channels. Compliance quality depends on whether intake, takedown, evidence retention, and recirculation controls are demonstrably operational, not merely documented. (Ref1)
statuses.penalty: sanctions are distributed across federal and state layers. Current corpus supports directionally stronger codification in non-consensual intimate-harm areas, but subtype-level penalty comparison still requires additional official state-law sources. (Ref1)

statuses.labeling: draft/proposal-stage and weak: the current Brazil materials support transparency and AI-governance analysis, but the corpus does not yet confirm an in-force general deepfake-labeling rule.
statuses.election: unclear/no dedicated rule in the current Brazil corpus: additional official election-law sources should be checked before coding an election-specific pathway.
statuses.non_consensual: unclear in the current corpus: the draft AI materials may be relevant to rights and harm prevention, but current files do not establish a dedicated NCII deepfake offense.
statuses.provider_liability: draft/proposal-stage and weak: the materials are relevant to accountability and governance, but should remain proposed until enacted obligations are confirmed.
statuses.penalty: draft/proposal-stage and weak: penalties or sanctions should not be coded as binding from these files alone.

statuses.labeling: current official resources do not clearly establish one comprehensive statutory deepfake-labeling mandate at article level. Guidance-level direction exists, so services should run proactive internal disclosure standards and keep update pathways ready for future hard-law clarification. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.election: explicit stand-alone election-deepfake codification is limited in current corpus. Election-risk handling should therefore remain conservative and be managed through broader cybercrime/public-order enforcement pathways. (Ref1)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual synthetic harms can be addressed through combined privacy, reputation, and unlawful dissemination provisions under cybercrime law. Given harm-speed dynamics, legal interpretation should be paired with immediate operational controls. (Ref1)
statuses.provider_liability: platform responsibility appears governed through general cybercrime enforcement architecture rather than a highly granular stand-alone platform-duty code. Services should demonstrate compliance through robust intake-decision-action-record chains, using guidance as operational support. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.penalty: sanctions are primarily applied through existing cybercrime penalties instead of deepfake-specific offense text. Practical penalty exposure therefore depends on accurate offense-category mapping during incident classification. (Ref1)

statuses.labeling: labeling obligations are visible in election online-advertising integrity context, but evidence remains limited for a broad all-domain statutory deepfake-labeling mandate. General service contexts should therefore be managed conservatively until additional official legal anchors are added. (Ref1)
statuses.election: election-related synthetic/deepfake controls are explicitly under legislative development through the amendment-bill pathway and represent the clearest codification track in current corpus. (Ref1)
statuses.non_consensual: current official corpus is insufficient to confirm a stand-alone dedicated non-consensual-deepfake offense framework. This dimension should remain conservative pending additional official criminal-law sources. (Ref1)
statuses.provider_liability: platform/intermediary duties are visible in election online-advertising integrity context, while broad deepfake platform-duty codification still needs further article-level confirmation. (Ref1)
statuses.penalty: sanctions are tied to election-integrity legislative pathway in current corpus; final sanction mapping should be refreshed after enacted-text confirmation. (Ref1)

statuses.labeling: current official resources do not clearly establish one comprehensive statutory deepfake-labeling mandate at article-level precision. However, multiple official guidance documents provide repeated transparency/labeling direction, so services should run internal disclosure standards now and keep rapid legal-update pathways for future binding-text changes. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3, Ref4, Ref5, Ref6)
statuses.election: explicit stand-alone election-deepfake codification remains limited in current corpus. Election-risk governance should be treated conservatively through broader cybercrime/public-order channels until dedicated official election-law anchors are available. (Ref1)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual deepfake harms appear addressable through combined privacy, reputation, and unlawful dissemination pathways under cybercrime law. Given harm-speed dynamics, legal interpretation should be paired with immediate operational controls. (Ref1)
statuses.provider_liability: platform responsibility appears to operate through general cybercrime enforcement architecture with guidance-layer supplementation, rather than a highly granular stand-alone platform-duty code. Compliance quality depends on end-to-end auditable intake-decision-action-record-cooperation chains. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3, Ref4, Ref5, Ref6)
statuses.penalty: sanctions are primarily applied through existing cybercrime penalties rather than deepfake-specific offense text. Practical exposure therefore depends on accurate offense-category mapping at incident intake. (Ref1)

statuses.labeling: guidance-level direction is present in current documents, but a clearly extracted deepfake-specific statutory labeling mandate is not yet confirmed in this corpus. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.election: dedicated election-deepfake codification is not clearly established in current local corpus. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.non_consensual: direct codification is not yet clearly established in this corpus; further official criminal-law sources are required. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.provider_liability: current corpus is centered on public-administration governance guidance rather than a dedicated platform-duty regime. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.penalty: sanction framework for deepfake-specific offenses remains unclear in currently loaded Argentina files. (Ref1, Ref2)

statuses.labeling: under current official local resources, OSA does not cleanly appear as one broad stand-alone deepfake-labeling clause for all contexts. Labeling implementation is instead reinforced through Ofcom/technical research materials, so teams should separate what is legally mandatory from what is operationally recommended. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3, Ref4)
statuses.election: explicit codification of a single election-deepfake-only statute remains limited in the current local corpus. Election-related claims should therefore be framed conservatively and cross-checked against election-specific legal instruments beyond the current OSA-centered set. (Ref1)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual intimate/sexual synthetic harms are treated as high-priority enforcement categories in practice. Effective compliance requires coupling legal policy with rapid operational response (intake, takedown, evidence retention, recirculation controls). (Ref1, Ref5)
statuses.provider_liability: this is the strongest codified area in the UK set. Statutory provider duties on risk management, harmful-content response, and enforcement cooperation are structured at law level, with guidance materials adding implementation detail. Compliance quality therefore depends on end-to-end workflow maturity, not policy text alone. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3, Ref5)
statuses.penalty: sanctions operate as a layered path across offense and enforcement channels, rather than one single penalty clause. Deepfake-subtype mapping still needs latest publication checks, but operationally the major risk driver is escalation after non-compliance with required corrective actions. (Ref1)

statuses.labeling: labeling direction is visible in official draft/explanatory materials, but a fully finalized nationwide comprehensive mandate is not yet certain from current corpus alone. Services should run proactive labeling controls now while keeping a legal-update mapping layer ready for final notification text and commencement details. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3)
statuses.election: explicit stand-alone election-deepfake codification remains limited in current resources. Election-risk handling should be managed conservatively through broader intermediary obligations and related public-communication/misinformation enforcement channels until dedicated official election-text coverage is expanded. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual deepfake harms appear governed through combined intermediary compliance and existing criminal-law pathways. Effective risk control requires immediate operational response controls (rapid restriction, evidence chain retention, recirculation suppression) alongside legal interpretation. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.provider_liability: this is the strongest currently codified dimension in India corpus. Intermediary obligations on intake, actioning, grievance process, and records are comparatively clear, so compliance quality depends on workflow consistency and auditability rather than policy text alone. (Ref1)
statuses.penalty: sanction pathways are distributed across existing legal channels, while deepfake-specific finalized article mapping remains in transition. Current best framing is: enforceable channels exist, but subtype-level sanction precision should be refreshed after final notification updates. (Ref1, Ref2)

statuses.labeling: unclear/no dedicated law in the current corpus: the AI Technology Act is relevant to AI governance, but the files do not establish a general deepfake disclosure or watermarking mandate.
statuses.election: no dedicated law identified in the current Japan corpus: election-specific synthetic-media rules require additional official sources.
statuses.non_consensual: adjacent-law pathway: APPI is relevant to personal information and privacy, but the current corpus does not establish a dedicated NCII deepfake offense.
statuses.provider_liability: adjacent/soft governance pathway: the current files support institutional AI-governance analysis, not a deepfake-specific platform-duty regime.
statuses.penalty: distributed/unclear: penalties should be linked to the specific adjacent law being applied, not coded as a standalone deepfake penalty regime.

statuses.labeling: labeling is one of the clearest codified dimensions in the China set. The 2022 provisions establish the baseline governance obligations, and the 2025 official notice strengthens implementation direction for generated/synthetic-content marking. Ref5 (proposed guidance) is non-final, so current-force conclusions should rely primarily on Ref1/Ref2/Ref4. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref4, Ref5)
statuses.election: current corpus does not provide clear evidence of one stand-alone election-deepfake statute. A conservative interpretation is that election-related synthetic harms are controlled through broader information-order, misinformation, and public-order regulatory channels. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual synthetic harms appear to be governed through combined administrative/public-order/cyber enforcement pathways rather than one isolated deepfake offense chapter in this corpus. Practical response still requires rapid operational controls (blocking, logging, cooperation requests) due to speed-of-harm dynamics. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.provider_liability: this is the structurally strongest codified area. Platform obligations on content management, labeling, identity/traceability, and removal/cooperation are embedded in administrative governance logic, making execution quality of internal workflows central to compliance outcomes. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref4)
statuses.penalty: sanctions follow a layered pathway combining administrative enforcement and criminal/public-order channels. Teams should manage exposure through stage-based enforcement logic (violation -> order -> non-compliance -> escalation), with latest-publication cross-checks for article-level precision. (Ref1, Ref2)

statuses.labeling: current official corpus does not clearly establish one nationwide comprehensive stand-alone deepfake-labeling mandate. Labeling expectations should be managed conservatively through existing cyber/platform channels, while keeping internal disclosure standards ready for rapid remapping when final text evolves. (Ref1, Ref2, Ref3)
statuses.election: explicit evidence of a stand-alone election-deepfake statute remains limited in current resources. Election-risk governance should therefore be handled through broader cybercrime/public-information control pathways until dedicated official election-law anchors are added. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual and harmful synthetic-content behavior appears addressable through combined criminal and platform-enforcement pathways in current corpus. Given harm-speed dynamics, immediate operational controls should accompany legal interpretation. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.provider_liability: platform obligations and authority-led removal/blocking pathways are comparatively clear in the amendment-centered structure. Compliance quality depends on whether intake-decision-action-record-cooperation chains are operational and auditable. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.penalty: sanctions are codified through cybercrime/platform governance channels, but final precision on authority scope, penalty depth, and commencement should be refreshed against latest promulgated text on a scheduled basis. (Ref1, Ref2)

statuses.labeling: current official corpus is insufficient to confirm a comprehensive nationwide stand-alone deepfake-labeling mandate at article-level precision. Labeling controls should be operated conservatively and updated when additional official text allows stronger clause-level confirmation. (Ref1)
statuses.election: explicit evidence of a stand-alone election-deepfake statute remains limited in the current set. Election-risk handling should be framed through broader digital-order/misinformation/public communication enforcement channels until dedicated official election-law sources are added. (Ref1)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual deepfake harms are best interpreted as handled through integrated criminal/digital-law pathways in current corpus context. Because direct article-level confirmation is limited, incident response should prioritize rapid harm mitigation controls while legal precision is further verified. (Ref1)
statuses.provider_liability: this is relatively the clearest dimension in current corpus. Service providers should maintain compliance-ready workflows for notice intake, actioning, record retention, and authority cooperation, with audit-grade traceability across the full process. (Ref1)
statuses.penalty: sanctions appear to operate through integrated existing legal channels; deepfake-specific penalty-article precision still requires additional official text verification. Current best framing is “enforcement path exists, fine-grained sanction precision pending.” (Ref1)

statuses.labeling: Korea’s labeling regime is dual-track. `Framework Act Article 31/32` sets baseline duties (advance notice and generated-output indication) for high-impact/generative AI services, while election context adds more operationally specific media-format requirements under `Annex 1-3`. In practice, this functions as “statutory baseline + context-specific format rules.” (Ref1, Ref4)
statuses.election: election governance is one of Korea’s most operationalized deepfake areas. Annex 1-3 provides implementable label items/display methods that can be translated directly into workflow checks for publication and circulation stages. The key caveat is that annex rules should be interpreted together with the broader election-law framework. (Ref4)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual deepfake harms are addressed through existing criminal/special-law enforcement channels rather than a stand-alone AI-framework offense chapter. The Information and Communications Network Act now gives a direct online-distribution anchor for AI-edited/synthesized/processed face, body, or voice material made against the subject's will, framed around prevention of sexual-crime, defamation, and fraud harms. Application remains category-specific and cross-statute. (Ref9)
statuses.provider_liability: provider liability is structurally split across three channels: high-impact AI governance duties under the Framework Act, consumer-protection enforcement under fair-labeling law, and network/platform response under the Information and Communications Network Act. The network-law track supports rights-protection duties, deletion/temporary blocking after requests, self-regulatory guidelines for unlawful information, and large-provider reporting/response architecture. (Ref2, Ref6, Ref10, Ref11)
statuses.penalty: sanctions follow a mixed pathway. Framework Act `Article 42/43` provides penalty/administrative-fine triggers, Fair Labeling Act `Article 7/18` supports corrective and punitive enforcement, and the Information and Communications Network Act adds penalties for non-compliance with regulator orders, unlawful-information distribution, joint penalty provisions, confiscation/collection, and administrative fines. Practical risk management should track first violation, takedown/temporary-blocking response, regulator order, and post-order non-compliance. (Ref3, Ref6, Ref7, Ref12)

statuses.labeling: based on current corpus, a single universal deepfake-labeling mandate is not cleanly separated as one broad statutory clause across all contexts. Labeling-related operational expectations are instead mediated through online-safety and industry-standard obligations, meaning implementation quality depends on policy-design and workflow controls rather than a single standalone labeling section. (Ref2, Ref3)
statuses.election: current official local materials provide limited direct evidence of one standalone federal election-deepfake statute. Election-related governance should therefore be stated conservatively and interpreted through interaction with broader online-safety and existing election/misinformation enforcement channels. (Ref2)
statuses.non_consensual: this is the strongest codified dimension in the Australian set. The 2024 criminal-code amendment provides explicit offense-strengthening for non-consensual sexual deepfake harms, making this area comparatively clear at federal criminal-law level. (Ref1, Ref4)
statuses.provider_liability: provider liability is operationally structured under the online-safety regime and associated standards. Effective compliance requires end-to-end controls for intake, classification, action, record-keeping, and reporting, not only high-level policy statements. (Ref2, Ref3)
statuses.penalty: sanctions are clearest in the non-consensual sexual deepfake track through criminal-law codification, while consolidated volumes help trace broader legal placement and amendment context. Operationally, teams should manage escalation risk across first breach, non-compliance with required actions, and repeat behavior. (Ref1, Ref4, Ref5, Ref6)

statuses.labeling: labeling/transparency is comparatively well codified at EU level. The AI Act provides baseline obligations for generated/manipulated content transparency, while DSA risk-mitigation duties reinforce practical distinguishability and user-facing accountability in platform contexts. This operates as a combined model of content-transparency duty plus platform-operational duty. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.election: no single stand-alone EU deepfake-election criminal statute is evident in current corpus. Governance is more realistically a combination of AI Act/DSA baseline obligations and member-state election-law enforcement, so election compliance must be jurisdiction-layered by member state. (Ref1, Ref2)
statuses.non_consensual: non-consensual deepfake criminal enforcement is primarily member-state criminal-law territory, while EU-level rules supplement through platform transparency, response, and data-protection obligations. Incident handling must therefore integrate both member-state criminal pathways and EU regulatory duties. (Ref1)
statuses.provider_liability: this is among the strongest codified dimensions in the EU set. DSA structures systemic risk governance, illegal-content response, and transparency reporting, and these obligations can intensify when combined with AI Act governance expectations. Practical compliance hinges on execution traceability, not policy text alone. (Ref2, Ref1)
statuses.penalty: EU does not provide one unified criminal deepfake penalty code; sanction exposure is layered across EU regulatory enforcement and member-state criminal channels. Effective risk management should track both EU-level and member-state-level sanction pathways simultaneously. (Ref1, Ref2)
